Ezekiel 36 confronts us with a God who acts not for our sake, but for His own name's sake—and this is meant to shake us into a deeper understanding of grace than we might prefer. The chapter opens with God's zeal burning against the nations that have mocked Israel and claimed her land as a prize. But notice what God fixes His attention on: not primarily the nation's suffering, but the reproach cast upon His own name. When Israel was scattered and humiliated, the watching nations concluded that Israel's God was weak, unable to protect His people or fulfill His promises. This was a direct assault on God's character. The restoration God promises—the reversal of desolation, the rebuilding of cities, the return of His people—is fundamentally about vindicating who He is. God's name is at stake, and He will not leave His reputation in the hands of those who mock Him. This is comfort, yes, but it is also a humbling reality: we are not the center of God's redemptive plan. His glory is.

Yet within this ruthless commitment to His own holiness lies the most astonishing promise: internal transformation. God will not simply return Israel to her land; He will give them a new heart and put His Spirit within them (verses 26-27). This is the heart of the gospel in the Old Testament. The people had defiled themselves through idolatry and bloodshed; they deserved continued judgment. But God promises to cleanse them completely—not because they deserve it, but because He will restore His name through their transformation. He will sprinkle clean water on them, remove their heart of stone, and cause them to walk in His statutes and observe His ordinances. This is not moral self-improvement or earned redemption. It is radical, unmerited restoration. God does what only God can do: He changes the human heart itself.

The chapter's repeated refrain—"It is not for your sake that I will act"—might seem cold until we understand what follows it. God acts for His name's sake by giving us new hearts and placing His Spirit within us. He acts for His glory by making us His people and becoming our God. This is the deepest security the redeemed can know: we are not loved because we are worthy or because we finally got our act together. We are loved because God's glory demands our transformation, and He Himself will accomplish it. The shame Ezekiel calls Israel to feel (verse 32) is not the shame of the condemned but of the humbled—those who recognize how far they fell and how far God went to bring them home. When you stand in the land that was once desolate, teeming with people and life again, you will know that the LORD has spoken and done it. This is the God who keeps His word not for our merit, but for the sake of His holy name.
